subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — EOG Practice Test 5
instructions: Read each selection carefully. Then answer the questions. Choose the one best answer for each question unless you are told to select more than one.

passages.title: The Lost Library Book
passages.text:
Part 1

Maya loved Tuesdays because Tuesday was library day. Each week she chose one book to take home, and each week she returned it on time. But this Tuesday was different. When Mr. Patel asked for her book about sea turtles, Maya reached into her backpack and found nothing but crumbs and a broken pencil.

Her cheeks turned warm. "I lost it," she whispered. Mr. Patel smiled kindly and told her not to worry, but Maya worried anyway. That book did not belong to her. It belonged to the whole school, and she had let everyone down.

All afternoon Maya thought about where the book could be. She had read it at the kitchen table. She had read it in the car. She had even read it under her blanket with a flashlight when she was supposed to be asleep.

Part 2

That evening, Maya decided to retrace her steps, just like a detective. She looked under the kitchen table. Nothing. She searched the back seat of the car. Nothing but an old sock. Then she remembered the flashlight and the blanket.

Maya raced to her bedroom and pulled back the covers. There, squished against the wall at the foot of her bed, was the sea turtle book. She had fallen asleep reading it, and it had slipped between the bed and the wall.

The next morning Maya carried the book to school as carefully as if it were made of glass. She handed it to Mr. Patel and apologized for the late return. "I'm proud of you for not giving up," he said. From that day on, Maya kept her library books in one special spot on her desk, where they could never disappear again.

passages.title: The Crow and the Pitcher's Cousin
passages.text:
Part 1

In a dry corner of the meadow, a thirsty rabbit named Bram hopped from stone to stone, searching for water. The sun had baked the ground hard, and every puddle had vanished. At last Bram found a tall, narrow jug resting in the shade of a fence post. He peered inside and saw a thin shine of water far down at the bottom.

Bram pushed his nose into the jug, but the opening was too small for his head. He tipped the jug, but it was heavy and would not budge. He even tried to knock it over with his strong back legs, but the jug only wobbled and stayed upright. Bram sat down, tired and discouraged.

Part 2

Just then a wise old crow landed on the fence post above him. "I once watched my cousin solve a puzzle just like this," the crow said. "Watch the ground, friend, not the jug."

Bram looked at the ground and saw it was scattered with small, round pebbles. He remembered the crow's words and began to think. One by one, he dropped pebbles into the jug. Plink. Plink. Plink. Slowly, the water crept higher and higher, until at last it reached the very top.

Bram drank deeply and thanked the crow. "You did the work yourself," the crow replied. "I only reminded you that a hard problem can be solved a small piece at a time." Bram hopped home that evening, no longer thirsty, and he never forgot the lesson of the pebbles.

passages.title: How Honeybees Make Honey
passages.text:
Part 1

Have you ever wondered where the honey on your toast comes from? It is made by honeybees, and the process begins far away in a field of flowers. A honeybee flies from flower to flower, drinking a sweet liquid called nectar. The bee stores the nectar in a special pouch inside its body, almost like a tiny backpack just for carrying food.

When the bee's pouch is full, it flies back to the hive. There it passes the nectar to other worker bees. These bees chew the nectar for a while, which changes it bit by bit. Then they spread the thick liquid into small wax rooms called cells.

Part 2

The nectar is still too watery to be honey, so the bees must dry it out. They beat their wings fast, like tiny fans, to blow air across the cells. As the air moves, the extra water disappears, and the nectar grows thick and sticky. Now it has finally become honey.

To keep the honey safe, the bees seal each full cell with a cap of wax. The honey can be stored this way for a long time. Bees make far more honey than they need so they will have plenty of food during the cold winter when no flowers bloom. Beekeepers gently take only the extra honey, leaving enough for the bees. The next time you taste honey, remember the long journey it took, from a flower to a hive to your table.

passages.title: Wilma Rudolph Runs to Gold
passages.text:
Part 1

When Wilma Rudolph was a little girl, very few people thought she would ever run. She was born in 1940 in Tennessee, one of many children in a large family. As a child she grew very sick with an illness that left her left leg weak. For years she wore a heavy brace and could not walk without help.

But Wilma was determined. Every day her mother and her brothers and sisters took turns rubbing her weak leg to help it grow stronger. Wilma practiced standing, then walking, a little more each day. By the time she was twelve years old, she surprised everyone by taking off the brace and walking on her own.

Part 2

Once Wilma could walk, she discovered she could run, and run fast. She joined her school track team and soon was winning race after race. People who had once felt sorry for her now cheered her name from the stands.

In 1960, Wilma traveled to the Olympic Games in Rome, Italy. There she raced against the fastest women in the world. When the games were over, Wilma had won three gold medals, becoming the first American woman ever to do so in a single Olympics. The girl who once could not walk had become one of the fastest runners on Earth. Wilma later returned home to teach and coach, showing young people that hard work and courage can carry them far.

passages.title: The Garden After Rain
passages.text:
Part 1

The rain has gone, the clouds have cleared,
The garden glows where gray appeared.
Each leaf is washed a brighter green,
The cleanest world I've ever seen.

A spider's web hangs near the gate,
Like beads of glass the raindrops wait.
They catch the sun and softly gleam,
A tiny string of silver dream.

Part 2

The robin hops across the lawn,
To greet the bright and dripping dawn.
He pulls a worm up from the ground,
Then tilts his head at every sound.

The flowers lift their drooping heads,
And stretch awake in muddy beds.
The whole world drank its morning cup,
And now the garden wakens up.

So let the rain come, soft and slow,
It helps the sleeping garden grow.
For after every cloudy day,
The sun returns to light the way.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what book had Maya borrowed from the library?
options: A book about cars
A book about detectives
A book about flashlights
A book about sea turtles
questions.explanation: Part 1 says Mr. Patel asked for her book about sea turtles, which is the book Maya had borrowed.

questions.questionText: What does the phrase "Her cheeks turned warm" help the reader understand about Maya?
options: She was feeling sick.
She was sitting too close to a fire.
She had been running fast.
She felt embarrassed and worried.
questions.explanation: Maya's cheeks turn warm right after she admits she lost the book, showing she felt embarrassed and worried.

questions.questionText: What does Maya decide to do that evening to find the book?
options: She asks Mr. Patel to help her search.
She retraces her steps like a detective.
She gives up and goes to sleep.
She buys a new book for the library.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says Maya decided to retrace her steps, just like a detective, to find the book.

questions.questionText: Where does Maya finally find the lost book?
options: Under the kitchen table
On her desk at school
Squished between her bed and the wall
In the back seat of the car
questions.explanation: Part 2 says the book was squished against the wall at the foot of her bed, where it had slipped between the bed and the wall.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes Maya in the story?
options: Careless
Responsible
Lazy
Unkind
questions.explanation: Maya worries about losing the school's book, searches until she finds it, and returns it carefully, which shows she is responsible.

questions.questionText: What is the lesson Maya learns in the story?
options: Library books are not very important.
You should never borrow books from school.
Not giving up and being careful can solve a problem.
It is best to read only at the kitchen table.
questions.explanation: Maya does not give up, finds the book, and afterward keeps her books in one safe spot. This shows that not giving up and being careful can solve a problem.

questions.questionText: Which detail from the text best shows that Maya was careful with the book after she found it?
options: "Her cheeks turned warm."
"She had read it in the car."
"Maya carried the book to school as carefully as if it were made of glass."
"All afternoon Maya thought about where the book could be."
questions.explanation: Carrying the book "as carefully as if it were made of glass" directly shows how careful Maya was with it.

questions.questionText: Select the two places Maya searched before she found the book.
options: Mr. Patel's office
The back seat of the car
Under the kitchen table
The school playground
questions.explanation: Part 2 says Maya looked under the kitchen table and searched the back seat of the car before remembering the flashlight and finding the book in her bed.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, why is Bram searching the meadow?
options: He is looking for a fence post.
He is looking for the crow.
He is looking for water.
He is looking for his cousin.
questions.explanation: Part 1 says the thirsty rabbit Bram was searching for water because the sun had dried up every puddle.

questions.questionText: What does the word "discouraged" mean as it is used in Part 1?
options: Feeling proud and happy
Wanting to run away quickly
Having lost hope after failing
Feeling thirsty and hot
questions.explanation: Bram sits down "tired and discouraged" after his tries to reach the water fail, so discouraged means having lost hope after failing.

questions.questionText: What does Bram do to make the water rise to the top of the jug?
options: He drops pebbles into the jug one by one.
He waits for rain to fill the jug.
He drinks the water with a long straw.
He tips the jug over on its side.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says Bram dropped pebbles into the jug one by one until the water crept up to the very top.

questions.questionText: What does Bram try first, before he uses the pebbles?
options: He pushes his nose into the jug and tries to tip it.
He goes home to rest.
He drinks from a nearby puddle.
He flies up to the fence post.
questions.explanation: In Part 1, Bram first pushes his nose into the jug and tries to tip it and knock it over, all before learning the pebble trick.

questions.questionText: What is the lesson of this story?
options: It is dangerous to be thirsty in a meadow.
Crows are smarter than rabbits.
A hard problem can be solved a small piece at a time.
You should never drink from a jug.
questions.explanation: The crow tells Bram that a hard problem can be solved a small piece at a time, which is the story's lesson, shown by the pebbles.

questions.questionText: What is one way the crow helps Bram?
options: The crow tips the heavy jug over for him.
The crow drops the pebbles into the jug for him.
The crow brings Bram water from another field.
The crow gives Bram a clue to look at the ground.
questions.explanation: The crow tells Bram to watch the ground, not the jug, which is the clue that leads Bram to the pebbles. The crow says Bram did the work himself.

questions.questionText: Use both parts of this text to answer this question. How does Bram change from the beginning to the end of the story?
options: He goes from friendly to angry.
He goes from thirsty and discouraged to satisfied and wiser.
He goes from helpful to selfish.
He goes from fast to slow.
questions.explanation: At the start Bram is thirsty and discouraged, but by the end he has drunk his fill and learned a lesson, so he is satisfied and wiser.

questions.questionText: What does the phrase "the sun had baked the ground hard" help the reader understand?
options: The ground was covered with bread.
The weather had been very hot and dry.
Someone was cooking food on the ground.
A fire had burned the meadow.
questions.explanation: Saying the sun "baked the ground hard" is a way of showing the weather had been very hot and dry, which is why the puddles vanished.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what sweet liquid do honeybees drink from flowers?
options: Water
Nectar
Wax
Honey
questions.explanation: Part 1 states that a honeybee drinks a sweet liquid called nectar from flower to flower.

questions.questionText: What do the worker bees do right after the flying bee brings nectar back to the hive?
options: They fly out to find more flowers.
They seal the cells with wax.
They beat their wings to dry it out.
They take the nectar and chew it for a while.
questions.explanation: Part 1 says the flying bee passes the nectar to other worker bees, who chew it for a while to begin changing it.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "cells" as it is used in the text?
options: Sweet liquid from flowers
Tiny pouches inside a bee's body
Fans that blow air
Small wax rooms where nectar is stored
questions.explanation: The text says the bees spread the nectar into small wax rooms called cells, so cells are small wax rooms.

questions.questionText: How do the bees turn the watery nectar into thick honey?
options: They add more water to it.
They beat their wings to blow air and dry it out.
They carry it back to the flowers.
They seal it in wax right away.
questions.explanation: Part 2 explains the bees beat their wings like fans to blow air across the cells, drying out the extra water until the nectar becomes thick honey.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in Part 1 and Part 2 of the text?
options: By describing the steps that turn nectar into honey, in order
By comparing two different kinds of bees
By telling a story about one beekeeper
By listing reasons people should fear bees
questions.explanation: Part 1 explains how bees gather and chew nectar, and Part 2 continues with drying and sealing it, giving the steps that make honey in order.

questions.questionText: According to the text, why do bees make far more honey than they need?
options: So they can build more cells
So the flowers will grow back
So beekeepers can sell it
So they will have food during the cold winter
questions.explanation: Part 2 says bees make extra honey so they will have plenty of food during the cold winter when no flowers bloom.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of the text?
options: Beekeepers take all of the honey from a hive.
Flowers cannot grow without bees.
Honeybees make honey through a series of steps that begin at a flower.
Honey tastes good on toast.
questions.explanation: The whole text explains how honeybees make honey step by step, starting with nectar from a flower.

questions.questionText: Which statement from the text best supports the idea that beekeepers are careful not to harm the bees?
options: "The honey can be stored this way for a long time."
"It is made by honeybees, and the process begins far away in a field of flowers."
"They beat their wings fast, like tiny fans..."
"Beekeepers gently take only the extra honey, leaving enough for the bees."
questions.explanation: The line about beekeepers gently taking only the extra honey and leaving enough for the bees supports the idea that they are careful not to harm the bees.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what did Wilma Rudolph have to wear for years as a child?
options: A gold medal
A heavy leg brace
A pair of running shoes
A team uniform
questions.explanation: Part 1 says Wilma wore a heavy brace for years and could not walk without help after her illness.

questions.questionText: What helped Wilma's weak leg grow stronger?
options: Her family took turns rubbing her leg every day.
She rested in bed for years.
She wore the brace all the time.
A doctor gave her medicine.
questions.explanation: Part 1 says her mother, brothers, and sisters took turns rubbing her weak leg every day to help it grow stronger.

questions.questionText: What does the word "determined" mean as it is used in Part 1?
options: Sick and weak
Set on reaching a goal and not giving up
Quiet and shy
Tired and sleepy
questions.explanation: Wilma practiced a little more each day until she could walk and run, so determined means being set on a goal and not giving up.

questions.questionText: What happened right after Wilma joined her school track team?
options: She traveled to Italy.
She grew very sick again.
She put her brace back on.
She soon began winning race after race.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says once Wilma joined the track team, she soon was winning race after race.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of the text?
options: Through hard work and courage, Wilma overcame illness to become a champion runner.
Track teams help children make new friends.
The Olympic Games take place in many countries.
Wilma Rudolph was born in Tennessee in 1940.
questions.explanation: The text as a whole shows how Wilma overcame a serious illness through hard work and courage to win three gold medals, which is its main idea.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in Part 1 and Part 2 of the text?
options: By describing the same race two times
By comparing two different runners
By showing how Wilma went from being unable to walk to becoming a champion
By listing rules of the Olympic Games
questions.explanation: Part 1 tells how Wilma could not walk, and Part 2 shows how she learned to run and won gold, connecting the two parts as a cause-and-effect change over time.

questions.questionText: Select the two facts the text gives about Wilma Rudolph's Olympic success.
options: She won three gold medals.
She refused to wear running shoes.
She set a world record that still stands.
She raced in Rome, Italy, in 1960.
questions.explanation: The text states that Wilma raced in Rome, Italy, in 1960 and won three gold medals. The other choices are not in the text.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes Wilma Rudolph, based on the text?
options: Courageous
Fearful
Careless
Forgetful
questions.explanation: Wilma kept working through a hard illness and never gave up, which shows she was courageous.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what has just happened at the start of the poem?
options: The rain has stopped and the clouds have cleared.
The sun has set for the night.
A storm is about to begin.
Snow has begun to fall.
questions.explanation: The poem opens with "The rain has gone, the clouds have cleared," showing the rain has just stopped.

questions.questionText: What does the poet mean by the phrase "a tiny string of silver dream" in Part 1?
options: Someone is dreaming about silver coins.
The spider has fallen asleep on the web.
The raindrops on the web shine and gleam like something magical.
A real string made of silver hangs on the gate.
questions.explanation: The phrase describes the raindrops on the spider's web catching the sun and gleaming, comparing them to a beautiful, dreamlike string of silver.

questions.questionText: In Part 2, what does the poet mean when the flowers "lift their drooping heads"?
options: The flowers are falling to the ground.
The flowers are perking up after the rain.
The flowers are closing for the night.
The flowers are being picked.
questions.explanation: After the rain, the flowers lift their drooping heads and stretch awake, which means they are perking up and reviving.

questions.questionText: What does the robin do in Part 2 of the poem?
options: It builds a nest in the apple tree.
It hops across the lawn and pulls a worm from the ground.
It sings a song to wake the flowers.
It drinks raindrops from the spider's web.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says the robin hops across the lawn and pulls a worm up from the ground.

questions.questionText: What is the main message of the poem?
options: Rain helps the garden grow, and brightness returns after every cloudy day.
Rain is something to be afraid of.
Spiders make the best webs after a storm.
Robins are the most important garden animals.
questions.explanation: The poem shows how the rain washes and wakens the garden, ending with the idea that the sun returns after every cloudy day, which is its main message.

questions.questionText: What does the line "The whole world drank its morning cup" help the reader picture?
options: The garden soaking up the rainwater to wake up fresh
A cup of water spilling on the flowers
People drinking tea in the garden
The robin drinking from a cup
questions.explanation: The line compares the rain-soaked garden to someone drinking a morning cup, picturing the garden taking in water and waking up fresh.

questions.questionText: How is the poem mainly organized?
options: By describing the garden as it comes alive after the rain
By telling about the four seasons of the year
By comparing two different gardens
By giving steps for planting a garden
questions.explanation: The poem moves through the garden after the rain, describing the leaves, web, robin, and flowers as the garden wakens up.

questions.questionText: Both "The Crow and the Pitcher's Cousin" and "The Garden After Rain" share which idea?
options: A person learns to run very fast.
A clever animal tricks another animal.
A long journey ends at someone's table.
Water brings a good change to a dry or sleeping world.
questions.explanation: In the fable, water saves the thirsty rabbit, and in the poem, rain wakens and refreshes the garden. Both show water bringing a good change. The other choices fit only one text or neither.
